Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Dismiss Action
Off Calendar May-23-2017 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. JOSHUA MARKOWITZ, TEL. NO. 1-650-207-1928 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. EMIL CHANG OF THE VENTURE PACIFIC LAW, TEL. NO. 1-650-567-3311 SPECIALLY APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT GLENDY SHIH'S Motion To Quash Service Of Summons And Dismiss Action - Defendant Glendy Shih's motion to quash service of summons on her is granted. Plaintiff Vincent Mini fails to demonstrate sufficient minimum contacts to impose California jurisdiction over Ms. Shih. Mr. Mini fails to show a specific contact that Ms. Shih made that is substantially connected to Mr. Mini's alleged injury. Ms. Shih is subject to specific jurisdiction if it is demonstrated that she purposefully directed her activities at California residents, purposefully derived a benefit from California activities, or purposefully availed herself of the privilege of conducting activities in California and the cause of action "arises out of" Ms. Shih's California contacts. The crucial inquiry concerns the character of Ms. Shih's activities in California, whether the case arises out of or has a substantial connection with those activities, and on the balancing of the convenience of the parties and the interests of California in assuming jurisdiction. (Vons Companies, Inc. v. Seabest Foods, Inc., (1996) 14 Cal.4th 434.) Mr. Mini blurs the distinction between Ms. Shih and the corporation. At most, he shows that Ms. Shih signed some corporate documents in California and Ms. Shih was a shareholder and/or officer of the corporation. "The act must be one for which the officer would be personally liable and the act must in fact create contact between the officer and the forum state. (For example, no personal contact would result from doing nothing more than ratifying an act taken by the corporation or by another corporate officer.)" (Seagate Technology v. A.J. Kogyo Co., 219 Cal.App.3d 696, 703-704.) Mr. Mini's stream of commerce theory fails because Ms. Shih is not vicariously liable for the corporation's contacts. The stream of commerce authorities cited by Mr. Mini do not discuss officer/director liability. Lastly, Mr. Mini's alter ego theory, which is not alleged in the complaint, fails because there is no showing of a unity of interest between Ms. Shih and the corporation. Order signed in open court. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR # 8189 =302/hk
